message:
feat(sqlmodel): add passive_deletes for relationship cascade control

PassiveDeletes Enum:
- Add PassiveDeletes enum with Active, Passive, and All variants
- Active (default): ORM emits DELETE for related objects
- Passive: Relies on database ON DELETE CASCADE, no DELETE emitted
- All: Passive + disables orphan tracking entirely

RelationshipInfo:
- Add passive_deletes field to control cascade behavior
- Add passive_deletes() builder method for fluent configuration
- Add is_passive_deletes() helper for Passive/All detection
- Add is_passive_deletes_all() helper for All-only detection

Macro Support:
- Parse passive_deletes attribute in relationship macro
- Support string values: "active", "passive", "all"

Schema & Session Integration:
- Update create.rs to handle passive delete configuration
- Update flush.rs to skip DELETE emission when passive
- Update change_tracker.rs for orphan tracking control

This mirrors SQLAlchemy's passive_deletes parameter, enabling efficient
database-side cascades without ORM overhead when ON DELETE CASCADE
is configured at the database level.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2pkb","title":"Implement Identity Map","description":"## Description

Implement identity map to ensure each DB row = one Rust object in session.

## What Identity Map Does

1. **Uniqueness**: Same PK always returns same object reference
2. **Cache**: Avoid redundant queries for same object
3. **Consistency**: Changes to object visible everywhere it's used

## Python Behavior

```python
user1 = session.get(User, 1)
user2 = session.get(User, 1)
assert user1 is user2  # Same object!

user1.name = 'Changed'
print(user2.name)  # 'Changed' - it's the same object
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
use std::any::TypeId;
use std::collections::HashMap;

pub struct IdentityMap {
    // TypeId -> (PrimaryKey -> Arc<RwLock<dyn Any>>)
    map: HashMap<TypeId, HashMap<PrimaryKeyValue, Arc<RwLock<Box<dyn Any>>>>>,
}

impl IdentityMap {
    pub fn get<M: Model + 'static>(&self, pk: &PrimaryKeyValue) -> Option<Arc<RwLock<M>>> {
        self.map
            .get(&TypeId::of::<M>())?
            .get(pk)?
            .clone()
            .downcast()
            .ok()
    }
    
    pub fn insert<M: Model + 'static>(&mut self, model: M) -> Arc<RwLock<M>> {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        let arc = Arc::new(RwLock::new(model));
        self.map
            .entry(TypeId::of::<M>())
            .or_default()
            .insert(pk, arc.clone());
        arc
    }
}
```

## Challenges in Rust

1. **Ownership**: Can't have multiple owners without Arc
2. **Mutability**: Need interior mutability (RwLock)
3. **Type erasure**: Need Any + downcast

## Alternative: Weak references

Use Weak<> to allow objects to be dropped when not in use:
```rust
map: HashMap<TypeId, HashMap<PrimaryKeyValue, Weak<RwLock<dyn Any>>>>
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Same PK returns same object
- [ ] Objects are reference-counted
- [ ] Modifications visible to all references
- [ ] Clear on session close
- [ ] Works with composite PKs

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/identity_map.rs)
- [ ] Test basic get/set operations
- [ ] Test same PK returns same object reference
- [ ] Test different objects with same data are identity-equal
- [ ] Test clear() removes all entries
- [ ] Test contains() for existing and non-existing keys
- [ ] Test memory leak prevention (weak references if applicable)

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_identity_map.rs)
- [ ] Query same row twice → same Rust object
- [ ] Modify via one reference → visible via other
- [ ] Session.add() with existing PK → proper conflict handling
- [ ] Concurrent access from multiple tasks
- [ ] Cross-session isolation (different sessions, different maps)

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Identity map hits/misses
- [ ] DEBUG: Object registration/eviction
- [ ] INFO: Map statistics on session close

 {"id":"bd-2px","title":"Implement exclude/include for field serialization","description":"## Description

Control which fields are included/excluded during serialization.

## Python Behavior

```python
class User(SQLModel):
    id: int
    password: str = Field(exclude=True)  # Never serialized
    email: str = Field(include={'dump'})  # Only in specific operations
```

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(exclude)]
    password: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(exclude_from = "json")]
    internal_field: String,
}
```

### Generated Code

Map to serde skip attributes:
- exclude → #[serde(skip_serializing)]
- exclude_from specific → conditional skip

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] exclude=True prevents field from appearing in model_dump
- [ ] Can exclude from specific serialization modes
- [ ] include works as whitelist
- [ ] Works with nested models

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/field.rs)
- [ ] Test exclude=True parsed
- [ ] Test include set parsed
- [ ] Test exclude set parsed
- [ ] Test nested field exclusion

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/field_exclude_include.rs)
- [ ] Field with exclude=True not in model_dump
- [ ] Field with include=[...] limits to list
- [ ] Field with exclude={...} omits from list
- [ ] Nested object exclusion
- [ ] Combine with by_alias

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Field exclusion decisions

-{"id":"bd-2q3","title":"Implement lazy loading for relationships","description":"## Description

Implement lazy loading semantics for relationships in Rust.

## Python Behavior

```python
class Team(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    heroes: List['Hero'] = Relationship(back_populates='team')

# Lazy loading - heroes loaded on first access
team = session.get(Team, 1)
print(team.heroes)  # SQL query executed HERE
```

## Rust Approach

Since Rust doesn't have implicit property access hooks, we use explicit loading:

### Option 1: LazyRelated<T> wrapper
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Team {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, lazy)]
    heroes: LazyRelated<Vec<Hero>>,
}

impl Team {
    async fn heroes(&self, cx: &Cx, session: &Session) -> Outcome<&[Hero], Error> {
        self.heroes.load(cx, session).await
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Session method
```rust
let heroes = session.load_related(cx, &team, Team::heroes).await?;
```

### Option 3: Auto-load configuration
```rust
#[sqlmodel(relationship, load = "auto")]
heroes: Related<Vec<Hero>>,
```

## Requirements

1. Track which relationships are loaded
2. Load on explicit request
3. Support batch loading to avoid N+1
4. Cache loaded relationships
5. Clear cache on refresh

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] LazyRelated<T> type implemented
- [ ] Load method fetches from database
- [ ] Loaded state tracked
- [ ] Works with Session context
- [ ] N+1 detection warns if many individual loads

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/lazy.rs)
- [ ] Test lazy relationship not loaded initially
- [ ] Test access triggers load
- [ ] Test loaded state persists
- [ ] Test lazy with session closed errors gracefully
- [ ] Test eager load override

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/lazy_loading.rs)
- [ ] Access hero.team → triggers SELECT on team
- [ ] Multiple access → single query
- [ ] Lazy load after detach → error
- [ ] Batch lazy loading (N+1 prevention)
- [ ] Lazy load with filters

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Lazy attribute access detected
- [ ] DEBUG: Lazy load query issued
- [ ] WARN: N+1 query pattern detected

 {"id":"bd-2sh","title":"Phase 10: Documentation and User Guides","description":"## Purpose
Create comprehensive documentation for the console integration, covering API reference, user guides, agent compatibility notes, and integration patterns.

## Background
Good documentation ensures:
- Users can quickly get started
- Advanced features are discoverable
- Agent authors understand compatibility
- Contributors understand the design

## Key Deliverables

### 1. API Documentation
- Rustdoc for all public types and traits
- Example code in doc comments
- Links between related items
- Feature flag documentation

### 2. Console User Guide
- Quick start (5-minute setup)
- Output mode configuration
- Theme customization
- Renderable catalog with examples

### 3. Agent Compatibility Guide
- How agent detection works
- Environment variables reference
- Stream separation semantics
- Writing agent-compatible code

### 4. Integration Patterns
- Attaching console to sessions
- Global vs explicit console
- Custom renderable creation
- Performance considerations

### 5. README Updates
- Update main sqlmodel README
- Add console feature section
- Include screenshots of output
- Link to detailed docs

## Documentation Locations
- crates/sqlmodel-console/README.md - crate overview
- docs/console/ - detailed guides
- Rustdoc in source files - API reference
- examples/ - runnable examples with comments

## Writing Standards
- Concise, action-oriented
- Code examples for everything
- Both rich and plain output shown
- Agent considerations always noted

## Dependencies
- All console features complete
- Visual examples finalized
- API stable

- All public items documented
- Examples in docs compile

 {"id":"bd-2ux","title":"Implement Session.refresh()","description":"## Description

Immediately reload object from database.

## Python Behavior

```python
# Reload all attributes
session.refresh(user)

# Reload specific attributes
session.refresh(user, ['name', 'email'])

# With options (eager load relationships)
session.refresh(user, with_for_update=True)
```

Unlike expire(), refresh() immediately queries the DB.

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub async fn refresh<M: Model>(
        &mut self,
        cx: &Cx,
        model: &mut M,
        attributes: Option<&[&str]>,
    ) -> Outcome<(), Error> {
        let pk = model.primary_key_value();
        
        // Query database
        let fresh: M = select!(M)
            .filter(M::primary_key().eq(pk))
            .one(cx, &self.conn)
            .await?;
        
        // Update model
        match attributes {
            Some(attrs) => model.copy_attributes_from(&fresh, attrs),
            None => *model = fresh,
        }
        
        // Update identity map
        self.identity_map.update(model);
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Immediately queries database
- [ ] Updates model with fresh values
- [ ] Can refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] Updates identity map
- [ ] Errors if object not in DB (was deleted)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/session/refresh.rs)
- [ ] Test refresh reloads from DB
- [ ] Test refresh with attribute subset
- [ ] Test refresh clears pending changes
- [ ] Test refresh updates identity map
- [ ] Test refresh with relationships

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/session_refresh.rs)
- [ ] Modify externally → refresh → sees new values
- [ ] refresh specific attributes only
- [ ] refresh with eager-loaded relationships
- [ ] refresh after expire
- [ ] refresh detached object fails

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Refresh query issued
- [ ] TRACE: Fields refreshed

-{"id":"bd-2x6","title":"Implement passive_deletes for relationships","description":"## Description

Support passive_deletes to let database handle cascade deletes.

## Python Behavior

```python
class Parent(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    children: List['Child'] = Relationship(
        back_populates='parent',
        passive_deletes=True  # Let DB handle deletes via FK ON DELETE
    )
    
    # With 'all', don't even warn about orphans
    children2: List['Child'] = Relationship(passive_deletes='all')
```

When passive_deletes=True:
- SQLAlchemy doesn't emit DELETE for children
- Relies on DB's ON DELETE CASCADE
- More efficient for large collections

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Parent {
    id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes)]
    children: Related<Vec<Child>>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship, passive_deletes = "all")]
    children2: Related<Vec<Child>>,
}
```

## Behavior

- passive_deletes=false (default): ORM deletes related objects
- passive_deletes=true: ORM relies on DB cascade
- passive_deletes='all': Don't even track orphans

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] passive_deletes attribute parsed
- [ ] Session.delete() respects passive_deletes
- [ ] Works with ON DELETE CASCADE FK constraint
- [ ] 'all' variant prevents orphan tracking

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/relationship.rs)
- [ ] Test passive_deletes=True parsed
- [ ] Test passive_deletes="all" parsed
- [ ] Test affects ON DELETE behavior
- [ ] Test with different FK constraints

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/passive_deletes.rs)
- [ ] Delete parent → DB cascades children (passive)
- [ ] passive_deletes="all" for all operations
- [ ] passive_deletes + ON DELETE CASCADE
- [ ] No orphan cleanup with passive
- [ ] Compare active vs passive delete behavior

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: passive_deletes configuration
- [ ] TRACE: Delete cascade behavior

 {"id":"bd-2ybr","title":"Implement built-in validators (uuid, ipv4, ipv6, mac_address)","description":"## Description

Add common validation patterns as built-in validators.

## Python Validators

```python
from pydantic import Field

class Network(SQLModel):
    device_id: str = Field(pattern=r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-...$')  # UUID pattern
    ipv4: str  # IPv4Address type does validation
    ipv6: str  # IPv6Address type does validation  
    mac: str = Field(pattern=r'^([0-9A-Fa-f]{2}:){5}[0-9A-Fa-f]{2}$')
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
struct Network {
    #[validate(uuid)]
    device_id: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv4)]
    ipv4: String,
    
    #[validate(ipv6)]
    ipv6: String,
    
    #[validate(mac_address)]
    mac: String,
    
    #[validate(slug)]
    url_slug: String,
    
    #[validate(credit_card)]
    card_number: String,
}
```

## Built-in Patterns

- uuid: UUID format
- ipv4: IPv4 address
- ipv6: IPv6 address
- mac_address: MAC address
- slug: URL-safe slug
- credit_card: Credit card number (Luhn check)
- phone: Phone number (E.164)
- hex_color: Hex color code

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] uuid validator
- [ ] ipv4 validator
- [ ] ipv6 validator
- [ ] mac_address validator
- [ ] slug validator
- [ ] credit_card validator (Luhn)
- [ ] Clear error messages for each

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/validate.rs)
- [ ] Test uuid validator accepts valid UUID
- [ ] Test uuid validator rejects invalid
- [ ] Test ipv4 validator
- [ ] Test ipv6 validator
- [ ] Test mac_address validator
- [ ] Test custom format validators

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/builtin_validators.rs)
- [ ] Field with uuid validation
- [ ] Field with ipv4 validation
- [ ] Field with ipv6 validation
- [ ] Field with mac_address validation
- [ ] Combined validators on one field
- [ ] Custom format registration

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Validator pattern matching
- [ ] TRACE: Validation failure details

 {"id":"bd-2z7a","title":"Implement dynamic model creation","description":"## Description

Create models dynamically at runtime.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlmodel import SQLModel, Field
from typing import Optional

def create_model(name: str, fields: dict):
    return type(name, (SQLModel,), {
        '__annotations__': fields,
        '__tablename__': name.lower(),
    })

DynamicUser = create_model('DynamicUser', {
    'id': Optional[int],
    'name': str,
})
```

## Rust Approach

Rust's static typing makes true dynamic models impossible, but we can:

### Option 1: Generic Dynamic Model
```rust
pub struct DynamicModel {
    table_name: String,
    columns: HashMap<String, ColumnDef>,
    values: HashMap<String, Value>,
}

impl DynamicModel {
    pub fn new(table_name: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn add_column(&mut self, name: &str, def: ColumnDef) { ... }
    pub fn set(&mut self, name: &str, value: Value) { ... }
    pub fn get(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&Value> { ... }
}
```

### Option 2: Schema from database
```rust
let schema = introspect_table(cx, conn, "users").await?;
let dynamic = DynamicModel::from_schema(schema);
```

## Use Cases

- Database introspection tools
- Generic admin interfaces
- Schema migration tools

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] DynamicModel type
- [ ] Runtime column definition
- [ ] Query execution with dynamic models
- [ ] Serialization to/from JSON
- [ ] Table creation from dynamic model

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/dynamic.rs)
- [ ] Test create_model function
- [ ] Test field definitions at runtime
- [ ] Test validators on dynamic models
- [ ] Test inheritance for dynamic models

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/dynamic_models.rs)
- [ ] create_model("User", [("name", str)]) works
- [ ] Dynamic model validates correctly
- [ ] Dynamic model serializes correctly
- [ ] Dynamic model in database operations
- [ ] Performance vs static models

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Dynamic model creation
- [ ] TRACE: Field definition processing

 {"id":"bd-3sqi","title":"Implement generic models support","description":"## Description

Support generic type parameters in models.

## Python Behavior

```python
from typing import TypeVar, Generic

T = TypeVar('T')

class Response(SQLModel, Generic[T]):
    data: T
    error: Optional[str] = None
    
# Usage
class UserResponse(Response[User]):
    pass

response = UserResponse(data=user)
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Response<T: Model> {
    data: T,
    error: Option<String>,
}

// Usage
type UserResponse = Response<User>;

let response = UserResponse {
    data: user,
    error: None,
};
```

2. Table names for generic models
3. Serialization with generic types

 {"id":"bd-3u8","title":"Implement schema extraction from Model definitions","description":"# Task: Implement Schema Extraction from Model Definitions

## Context
To compare against the database, we need to extract the "expected" schema from Rust Model definitions at compile time or runtime.

## Goal
```rust
// From these Model definitions...
#[derive(Model)]
struct Hero {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(sql_type = "VARCHAR(100)")]
    name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(nullable)]
    age: Option<i32>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(foreign_key = "teams.id", on_delete = "CASCADE")]
    team_id: Option<i64>,
}

// ...extract this schema:
TableSchema {
    name: "heroes",
    columns: [
        ColumnSchema { name: "id", sql_type: "BIGINT", nullable: false, auto_increment: true },
        ColumnSchema { name: "name", sql_type: "VARCHAR(100)", nullable: false },
        ColumnSchema { name: "age", sql_type: "INTEGER", nullable: true },
        ColumnSchema { name: "team_id", sql_type: "BIGINT", nullable: true },
    ],
    primary_key: Some(PrimaryKeySchema { columns: ["id"] }),
    foreign_keys: [
        ForeignKeySchema { 
            columns: ["team_id"], 
            referenced_table: "teams",
            referenced_columns: ["id"],
            on_delete: Some("CASCADE"),
        }
    ],
    indexes: [...],
}
```

## What to Implement

### 1. Model::table_schema() Method
```rust
// Add to Model trait
pub trait Model {
    // ... existing ...
    
    /// Get the expected table schema for this model.
    fn table_schema() -> TableSchema;
}
```

### 2. Generate table_schema() in Macro
```rust
// In sqlmodel-macros/src/lib.rs

fn generate_table_schema(def: &ModelDef) -> TokenStream {
    let table_name = &def.table_name;
    
    let columns: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter().map(|f| {
        let name = &f.column_name;
        let sql_type = &f.effective_sql_type();
        let nullable = f.nullable;
        let auto_inc = f.auto_increment;
        let default = f.default.as_ref().map(|d| quote!(Some(#d.to_string())))
            .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
        
        quote! {
            sqlmodel_schema::ColumnSchema {
                name: #name.to_string(),
                sql_type: #sql_type.to_string(),
                nullable: #nullable,
                default: #default,
                auto_increment: #auto_inc,
                comment: None,
                parsed_type: sqlmodel_schema::parse_sql_type(#sql_type),
            }
        }
    }).collect();
    
    let pk_columns: Vec<String> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter(|f| f.primary_key)
        .map(|f| f.column_name.clone())
        .collect();
    
    let pk = if pk_columns.is_empty() {
        quote!(None)
    } else {
        quote! {
            Some(sqlmodel_schema::PrimaryKeySchema {
                name: None,
                columns: vec![#(#pk_columns.to_string()),*],
            })
        }
    };
    
    let fks: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter_map(|f| f.foreign_key.as_ref().map(|fk| (f, fk)))
        .map(|(f, fk)| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            let (ref_table, ref_col) = parse_fk_reference(fk);
            let on_delete = f.on_delete.as_ref()
                .map(|d| quote!(Some(#d.to_string())))
                .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
            let on_update = f.on_update.as_ref()
                .map(|u| quote!(Some(#u.to_string())))
                .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
            
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::ForeignKeySchema {
                    name: None,
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                    referenced_table: #ref_table.to_string(),
                    referenced_columns: vec![#ref_col.to_string()],
                    on_delete: #on_delete,
                    on_update: #on_update,
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    let indexes: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter_map(|f| f.index.as_ref().map(|idx| (f, idx)))
        .map(|(f, idx_name)| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::IndexSchema {
                    name: #idx_name.to_string(),
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                    unique: false,
                    index_type: None,
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    // Unique constraints
    let uniques: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter(|f| f.unique)
        .map(|f| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::UniqueConstraintSchema {
                    name: None,
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    quote! {
        fn table_schema() -> sqlmodel_schema::TableSchema {
            sqlmodel_schema::TableSchema {
                name: #table_name.to_string(),
                columns: vec![#(#columns),*],
                primary_key: #pk,
                foreign_keys: vec![#(#fks),*],
                unique_constraints: vec![#(#uniques),*],
                indexes: vec![#(#indexes),*],
                check_constraints: vec![],
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### 3. Aggregate Multiple Models
```rust
/// Build a complete expected schema from multiple models.
pub fn expected_schema<M: Model>() -> DatabaseSchema {
    let mut schema = DatabaseSchema::new(Dialect::default());
    schema.tables.insert(M::table_name().to_string(), M::table_schema());
    schema
}

/// Build schema from a tuple of models.
pub fn expected_schema_all<Models: ModelTuple>() -> DatabaseSchema {
    let mut schema = DatabaseSchema::new(Dialect::default());
    for table in Models::all_table_schemas() {
        schema.tables.insert(table.name.clone(), table);
    }
    schema
}

// Trait for tuples of models
pub trait ModelTuple {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema>;
}

// Implement for tuples
impl<A: Model, B: Model> ModelTuple for (A, B) {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema> {
        vec![A::table_schema(), B::table_schema()]
    }
}

impl<A: Model, B: Model, C: Model> ModelTuple for (A, B, C) {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema> {
        vec![A::table_schema(), B::table_schema(), C::table_schema()]
    }
}

// ... more tuple impls via macro
```

### 4. Handle Type Mapping Variations
SQL types vary by dialect:
```rust
impl ColumnSchema {
    /// Normalize the SQL type for comparison.
    /// e.g., "INT" and "INTEGER" are equivalent.
    pub fn normalized_type(&self, dialect: Dialect) -> String {
        let upper = self.sql_type.to_uppercase();
        
        match dialect {
            Dialect::Sqlite => {
                // SQLite type affinity
                if upper.contains("INT") { "INTEGER".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("CHAR") || upper.contains("TEXT") { "TEXT".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("REAL") || upper.contains("FLOAT") || upper.contains("DOUBLE") { "REAL".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("BLOB") { "BLOB".to_string() }
                else { upper }
            }
            Dialect::Postgres => {
                // PostgreSQL normalizations
                match upper.as_str() {
                    "INT" | "INT4" => "INTEGER".to_string(),
                    "INT8" => "BIGINT".to_string(),
                    "INT2" => "SMALLINT".to_string(),
                    "FLOAT4" => "REAL".to_string(),
                    "FLOAT8" => "DOUBLE PRECISION".to_string(),
                    _ => upper,
                }
            }
            Dialect::Mysql => {
                // MySQL normalizations
                match upper.as_str() {
                    "INT" => "INT".to_string(),
                    "INTEGER" => "INT".to_string(),
                    _ => upper,
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## Files to Modify
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/lib.rs` (generate table_schema())
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/model.rs` (add to trait)
- Create: `crates/sqlmodel-schema/src/expected.rs` (helpers)

## Testing

- Test with all field attributes (pk, fk, unique, index, etc.)
- Test type normalization across dialects
- Test composite primary keys
- Test ModelTuple aggregation

- [ ] All column attributes reflected in schema
- [ ] FK on_delete/on_update captured
- [ ] Indexes and unique constraints captured
- [ ] Type normalization for comparison
- [ ] ModelTuple for multiple models

 {"id":"bd-3w42","title":"Implement model-level validators","description":"## Description

Validators that run on entire model, not just individual fields.

## Python Behavior

```python
from pydantic import model_validator

class User(SQLModel):
    password: str
    confirm_password: str
    
    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def passwords_match(self) -> Self:
        if self.password != self.confirm_password:
            raise ValueError('passwords do not match')
        return self
    
    @model_validator(mode='before')
    @classmethod
    def preprocess(cls, data: dict) -> dict:
        # Can modify data before field validation
        return data
```

## Rust Implementation

### Option 1: Trait method
```rust
pub trait ModelValidate {
    fn validate_model(&self) -> Result<(), ValidationError> {
        Ok(())
    }
}

impl ModelValidate for User {
    fn validate_model(&self) -> Result<(), ValidationError> {
        if self.password != self.confirm_password {
            return Err(ValidationError::new("passwords do not match"));
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Attribute on impl
```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
#[validate(model = "validate_passwords")]
struct User {
    password: String,
    confirm_password: String,
}

impl User {
    fn validate_passwords(&self) -> Result<(), String> {
        if self.password != self.confirm_password {
            return Err("passwords do not match".into());
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Modes

- **before**: Run before field validation, can modify input
- **after**: Run after field validation, validates relationships
- **wrap**: Wrap field validation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] model_validator equivalent implemented
- [ ] Runs after field validation
- [ ] Can access all fields
- [ ] Can return error
- [ ] 'before' mode preprocesses input
- [ ] 'after' mode validates result

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/validate_derive.rs)
- [ ] Test model_validator mode="after" parsed
- [ ] Test model_validator mode="before" parsed
- [ ] Test validator receives full model
- [ ] Test validator can modify model

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/model_validators.rs)
- [ ] mode="after" runs after field validation
- [ ] mode="before" preprocesses input
- [ ] Validator can raise error
- [ ] Multiple model validators
- [ ] Cross-field validation (password confirm)

- [ ] TRACE: Validation mode

-{"id":"bd-3xk","title":"Implement Session.expunge_all()","description":"## Description

Remove all objects from session without deleting from database.

## Python Behavior

```python
session.expunge_all()  # All objects detached
```

After expunge_all:
- Objects become detached
- Changes won't be persisted
- Objects can be merged back later

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub fn expunge_all(&mut self) {
        self.identity_map.clear();
        self.new.clear();
        self.dirty.clear();
        self.deleted.clear();
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All objects removed from session tracking
- [ ] Identity map cleared
- [ ] Pending changes discarded (but not reverted in objects)
- [ ] Objects can be merged back into new session

 {"id":"bd-53s","title":"Implement Decimal precision validation (max_digits, decimal_places)","description":"## Description

Add full Decimal type support with precision and scale validation.

## Python Behavior

```python
from decimal import Decimal

class Product(SQLModel, table=True):
    price: Decimal = Field(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)
    # SQL: DECIMAL(10, 2) or NUMERIC(10, 2)
```

## Current Status

We have a workaround using sql_type attribute:
```rust
#[sqlmodel(sql_type = "DECIMAL(10,2)")]
price: Decimal,
```

But need native support matching Python.

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
struct Product {
    #[sqlmodel(max_digits = 10, decimal_places = 2)]
    price: rust_decimal::Decimal,
}
```

### SQL Generation
Generate correct DDL:
- PostgreSQL: NUMERIC(10, 2)
- MySQL: DECIMAL(10, 2)
- SQLite: TEXT (with validation)

### Validation
- Check total digits <= max_digits
- Check decimal places <= decimal_places

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] max_digits and decimal_places parsed from attributes
- [ ] Correct SQL type generated for each dialect
- [ ] Validation enforces constraints
- [ ] Works with rust_decimal crate

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/field.rs)
- [ ] Test max_digits parsed correctly
- [ ] Test decimal_places parsed correctly
- [ ] Test validation rejects too many digits
- [ ] Test validation rejects too many decimal places
- [ ] Test edge cases (0, negative, very large)

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/decimal_validation.rs)
- [ ] price with max_digits=10, decimal_places=2
- [ ] Valid: 12345678.99 (10 digits, 2 decimal)
- [ ] Invalid: 123456789.99 (11 digits)
- [ ] Invalid: 12345678.999 (3 decimal places)
- [ ] Database roundtrip preserves precision

### Logging

- [ ] TRACE: Digit counting

 {"id":"bd-59d","title":"Create SchemaTree renderable for database structure visualization","description":"## Purpose
Implement a tree renderable that displays database schema structure in a hierarchical format, perfect for visualizing table relationships, columns, indexes, and constraints.

## Background
When users inspect database schemas, they need to see:
- Tables as tree nodes
- Columns with types and constraints as children
- Indexes and foreign keys as nested items
- Visual distinction between primary keys, nullable, indexed columns

This uses rich_rust's Tree component to create an intuitive visual representation.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
`crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/schema_tree.rs`

### Core Struct
```rust
use rich_rust::prelude::*;
use sqlmodel_schema::{TableSchema, ColumnInfo, IndexInfo, ForeignKey};

/// Configuration for schema tree rendering
pub struct SchemaTreeConfig {
    /// Show column types inline
    pub show_types: bool,
    /// Show constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL)
    pub show_constraints: bool,
    /// Show indexes
    pub show_indexes: bool,
    /// Color theme
    pub theme: Theme,
}

impl Default for SchemaTreeConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            show_types: true,
            show_constraints: true,
            show_indexes: true,
            theme: Theme::default(),
        }
    }
}

/// Renders a database schema as a tree structure
pub struct SchemaTree<'a> {
    tables: &'a [TableSchema],
    config: SchemaTreeConfig,
    title: Option<String>,
}

impl<'a> SchemaTree<'a> {
    pub fn new(tables: &'a [TableSchema]) -> Self {
        Self {
            tables,
            config: SchemaTreeConfig::default(),
            title: None,
        }
    }

    pub fn with_config(mut self, config: SchemaTreeConfig) -> Self {
        self.config = config;
        self
    }

    pub fn title(mut self, title: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.title = Some(title.into());
        self
    }

    /// Build tree node for a single table
    fn build_table_node(&self, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let table_label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.table_name_color(),
            table.name
        );
        let mut node = TreeNode::new(&table_label);

        // Add columns
        for col in &table.columns {
            let col_node = self.build_column_node(col, table);
            node.add_child(col_node);
        }

        // Add indexes if enabled
        if self.config.show_indexes {
            for idx in &table.indexes {
                let idx_node = self.build_index_node(idx);
                node.add_child(idx_node);
            }
        }

        node
    }

    fn build_column_node(&self, col: &ColumnInfo, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let mut parts = vec\![col.name.clone()];

        if self.config.show_types {
            parts.push(format\!(
                "[{}]{}[/]",
                self.config.theme.type_color(),
                col.sql_type
            ));
        }

        if self.config.show_constraints {
            let mut constraints = Vec::new();
            if col.is_primary_key {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]PK[/]",
                    self.config.theme.primary_key_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!col.nullable {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]NOT NULL[/]",
                    self.config.theme.constraint_color()
                ));
            }
            if col.has_default {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]DEFAULT[/]",
                    self.config.theme.default_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!constraints.is_empty() {
                parts.push(constraints.join(" "));
            }
        }

        TreeNode::new(&parts.join(" "))
    }

    fn build_index_node(&self, idx: &IndexInfo) -> TreeNode {
        let idx_type = if idx.unique { "UNIQUE" } else { "INDEX" };
        let label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/] {} ({})",
            self.config.theme.index_color(),
            idx_type,
            idx.name,
            idx.columns.join(", ")
        );
        TreeNode::new(&label)
    }

    /// Render to segments for rich mode
    pub fn render(&self, width: usize) -> Vec<Segment> {
        let root_label = self.title.clone()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "Database Schema".to_string());
        let mut root = TreeNode::new(&format\!(
            "[bold {}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.heading_color(),
            root_label
        ));

        for table in self.tables {
            root.add_child(self.build_table_node(table));
        }

        let tree = Tree::new(root);
        tree.render(width)
    }

    /// Render to plain text for agent mode
    pub fn render_plain(&self) -> String {
        let mut output = String::new();
        let title = self.title.as_deref().unwrap_or("Database Schema");
        output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", title));
        output.push_str(&"=".repeat(title.len()));
        output.push(n);

        for table in self.tables {
            output.push_str(&format\!("\nTable: {}\n", table.name));
            for col in &table.columns {
                let mut line = format\!("  - {}: {}", col.name, col.sql_type);
                if col.is_primary_key {
                    line.push_str(" [PK]");
                }
                if \!col.nullable {
                    line.push_str(" NOT NULL");
                }
                output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", line));
            }
        }
        output
    }
}
```

## Usage Example
```rust
let tables = vec\![user_schema, post_schema];
let tree = SchemaTree::new(&tables)
    .title("my_database")
    .with_config(SchemaTreeConfig {
        show_indexes: true,
        ..Default::default()
    });

console.print_renderable(&tree);
```

## Visual Output (Rich Mode)
```
📊 my_database
├── users
│   ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
│   ├── name TEXT NOT NULL
│   ├── email TEXT NOT NULL
│   └── UNIQUE idx_users_email (email)
└── posts
    ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
    ├── user_id INTEGER NOT NULL
    ├── title TEXT NOT NULL
    └── INDEX idx_posts_user (user_id)
```

## Verification Steps
1. Create test schema with multiple tables
2. Verify tree renders correctly in rich mode
3. Verify plain text output for agent mode
4. Test with various config options
5. Ensure colors match Theme settings
6. Test with empty schema (no tables)
7. Test with table with no indexes

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-schema for TableSchema, ColumnInfo types
- rich_rust Tree and TreeNode components
